title: Young life: Why gen Z women should ditch the 'office siren' look
gist: In a personal essay, Prospect writer Alice Garnett argues gen Z women should retire the sexualized 'office siren' look — once dubbed 'corporate fetish' — in favor of contextually appropriate but still individually expressive workplace style, using her own hybrid-office wardrobe (colorful pieces plus a tooth gem) as her case for a 'balanced approach.'

insights.claim: Garnett argues the 'office siren' trend among gen Z women (mini-skirts, stockings, cinched waists, heavy accessorizing), previously dubbed 'corporate fetish,' fails the basic fashion test of dressing appropriately for the occasion, even though gen Z women are right to challenge being judged by skirt length.
insights.speaker: Alice Garnett
insights.so_what: Signals a shift in gen Z workplace-fashion sentiment away from the hyper-sexualized 'office siren' trend toward more contextually toned-down individuality — relevant for workwear and fashion retailers tracking generational style pivots.
insights.quote: the “office siren trend” fails to pass the most basic fashion test: are you dressing for the occasion?

insights.claim: Garnett rejects a purely individualist reading of gen Z workplace-authenticity culture, arguing it is naive and self-centred to believe one should be able to wear whatever they want at work regardless of setting, circumstance, and audience.
insights.speaker: Alice Garnett
insights.so_what: A counter-narrative to the 'wear what you want' strand of gen Z workplace discourse — useful context for HR and dress-code policy debates as employers manage younger staff returning to offices.
insights.quote: It is naive (and somewhat self-centred) to believe that you should be able to wear whatever you want, regardless of setting, circumstance and audience.

insights.claim: Garnett observes that how women dress for work remains politicized, with women judged and criticised for their appearance regardless of what they actually wear.
insights.speaker: Alice Garnett
insights.so_what: Frames ongoing gender-based scrutiny in professional settings as a structural issue independent of specific fashion trends — relevant to workplace-culture and DEI discourse.
insights.quote: women are judged and criticised for how they look, regardless of what they wear
insights.locator: How women dress for work is still

insights.claim: Garnett frames Canary Wharf's hyper-professional environment and business jargon ('circle back', 'BAU', 'synergy') as culturally alienating to gen Z newcomers who prioritize personal authenticity.
insights.speaker: Alice Garnett
insights.so_what: Highlights a generational friction point employers may need to address in office-return and culture policies as they pull younger staff back into hybrid or in-office roles.
insights.quote: This financial district is one of the places in London you would least expect to find me.
insights.locator: This financial district is one of the places

insights.claim: Garnett describes combining an office-appropriate silhouette with personally expressive colors and a tooth gem as a low-risk form of workplace self-expression that drew office compliments rather than any HR issue.
insights.speaker: Alice Garnett
insights.so_what: A case study in low-risk personal branding at work — relevant to consumer-trend watchers tracking accessible self-expression products (tooth gems, colored accessories) as a retail micro-trend.
insights.quote: A tooth gem feels like a very small act of aesthetic rebellion in the grey cityscape that is Canary Wharf
insights.locator: So far, I've received nothing but compliments
